variants.llm_generated: The federal death tax, officially known as the estate tax, is a tax levied by the United States government on the transfer of a deceased person's taxable estate. This tax applies to the value of the estate transferred to heirs or beneficiaries, whether through a will, state intestacy laws, trusts, or other means, and can also include certain life insurance payouts or financial accounts distributed upon the owner's death.
variants.llm_humanized: So, the federal death tax—more formally called the estate tax—is basically a tax the U.S. government charges when someone passes away and their assets get transferred to their heirs. It covers things like property, money, or even life insurance payouts, whether those are passed on through a will, a trust, or just by default if there’s no will.
variants.human_polished: The estate tax in the United States is a tax imposed on the transfer of a deceased person's taxable estate. This applies whether the property is transferred through a will, according to state intestacy laws, or by other means, such as trusts or the payment of certain life insurance benefits or financial accounts to beneficiaries.
variants.dictated_spoken: Yeah so the federal death tax, or estate tax, is basically a tax that gets charged when someone dies and their stuff gets passed on to other people. It doesn’t really matter if it’s through a will or if there’s no will and the state decides, or even if it’s from a trust or life insurance payout—if it’s part of the person’s estate, it can be taxed.
